turns.content: I've been using the standard test runner for years, but I'm trying to add a custom coverage tool like cargo-llvm-cov and I keep getting confused about how it fits into my project structure. It feels very similar to how I used to run tests in Python with pytest, except this one seems to require specific flags when invoking the compiler directly.

That's a great way to think about it; cargo-llvm-cov acts like a bridge between your Rust compiler output and coverage data. Unlike standard tests that just verify behavior, this tool analyzes the compiled binaries to see which lines were executed. You need to ensure you're actually compiling with debug symbols enabled because without them, the coverage report won't have enough information. The key here is understanding how cargo-llvm-cov consumes the LLVM IR and maps it back to your source files.

I think I understand the concept now, but when I try to generate an HTML report, it seems like the tool isn't finding my test functions. Is there a way to exclude specific modules or just run all tests automatically?

The tool does support automatic discovery of test binaries if you're using the right crate configuration, but sometimes it's tricky because Rust doesn't always distinguish between library code and test code in the same way Python does. You can use exclude patterns to filter out specific crates or modules that shouldn't be counted toward your coverage percentage. For example, you might want to skip testing internal helper utilities while still counting on user-facing functionality.

That makes sense; I was wondering if there's a way to integrate this into my CI pipeline so it runs automatically after every commit. Does cargo-llvm-cov have any built-in support for that, or do I need to add extra steps?

You don't necessarily need extra steps if you configure your environment correctly, but the most straightforward approach is to run cargo test followed by the coverage command in your CI script. The important part is ensuring that your build process includes the necessary debug symbols and that the coverage output directory doesn't get cleaned up prematurely. This way, each successful build can generate a fresh HTML report for you to review.
